categories.label: Analog and Mixed Signal
categories.description: Circuit-level work including amplifiers, filters, converters, measurement front ends, power stages, and mixed-signal interfaces.

categories.label: Digital
categories.description: Digital design projects involving logic design, FPGA workflows, verification, timing, and ASIC-oriented architecture.

categories.label: Embedded
categories.description: MCU-based systems with firmware, board bring-up, interfaces, sensing, control, and hardware/software integration.

funFacts: I built the builder to create this portfolio, letting me add text and files while parsing the provided input.
I enjoy playing soccer, especially as a defensive midfielder.
I also enjoy a good debate about the game, whether it’s tactics, positional awareness, or the way players carry themselves on the field.

projects.title: PWM Generating Voltage Controlled oscillator
projects.templateLabel: Clean white, blue hover, white click

templateVisual.interaction: Blue hover with a clean white click state.

projects.summary:
In many systems, timing circuits are needed not only for synchronous digital design but also to control mixed-signal circuits and interface functions.   A few examples of clocked analog circuits include analog-to-digital converters, digital-to-analog converters, switched-capacitor filters, and chopper-stabilized op-amps.  A single IC may require multiple clock frequencies for its analog and digital cells.  Since 
these clocks may not be related by simple integer ratios and created with dividers, voltage-controlled oscillators (VCO) are popular circuits to generate the required frequencies on chip.  In this project, I analyze, design, and characterize a VCO topology and use it to drive an LED via a PWM output.

In this project, I analyze, design, and characterize a VCO topology and use it to drive an LED via a PWM output.

projects.title: Smart Technology Rollator for Independence, Detection, and Empowerment (STRIDE)
projects.templateLabel: Clean white, blue hover, white click

templateVisual.interaction: Blue hover with a clean white click state.

projects.summary:
Abstract

The Lafayette College Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering requested that the class of 2026 design a STRIDE system. Mobility devices are designed to meet
the needs of their users, and there is a recognized difficulty in navigating stairs with most
mobility devices; additionally, there is a recognized potential advantage in incorporating
obstacle detection systems to aid in navigation tasks. Thus, the senior design team developed
a device that encompasses three systems: a rollator, a stair-climbing mechanism, and a sensor
detection system.

The completed system is a motor-driven rollator with the ability to navigate both flat
ground and stairs. This system is comprised of: an aluminum tubing frame with aluminum
and acrylic paneling; four tri-wheel assemblies, two of which are powered and interconnected
by gear-chain systems, the other two composed of omnidirectional wheels; two DC brushless
motors, powering the aforementioned tri-wheels; a 24 V DC battery and a custom power routing system; an obstacle-detection system; a physical interface; a remote control interface;
and an MCU hub. The rollator can operate under direct user control through a set of physical
buttons and braking mechanisms, as well as autonomously through a remote control terminalon a password-protected website. It supports continuous operation on flat ground for up
to 8 hours before recharge. The system is to be used indoors only on ADA-compliant
staircases and surfaces. This document provides an introductory problem statement; the
design requirements and considerations; the evolution of the design; the final details of the
design; an overview of the testing and evaluation process; conclusions and future work; and
an overview of team management.

Introduction

Enhancing an individual’s mobility is crucial for supporting independence and empowerment among seniors and individuals who have lost mobility due to injury or illness. In
recent years, designers and companies have recognized the need expressed by these people for
mobility support devices that are specifically tailored to an individual’s level of mobility; canes
and wheelchairs are both useful, potentially life-changing devices, but are only appropriate
for certain users. The emphasis on designing devices aligned with users’ variable needs has
resulted in the popularization of the rollator, which provides a level of support between a
cane and a wheelchair.

A typical rollator is a four-wheeled device with handles, used to support a user’s balance
when navigating flat or moderately sloped terrain. It is more mobile on flat ground than
a walker, but can become more burdensome on sloped terrain, where it either resists the
motion of the user when going uphill or threatens to run away from the user when going
downhill. It is bulky and hard to carry up or down stairs, especially for target users. Sloped
surfaces and elevation changes, especially with an added load, are some of the first obstacles
that people begin to have trouble with as they age or lose mobility. Additionally, typical
rollators do not provide any assistance with navigation for users, who might have impaired
vision or be experiencing cognitive decline.

Thus, a niche can be identified within the existing scope of mobility devices for a rollator
that can better support users on sloped terrain; that can traverse stairs, removing the need
to lift it up and down; that can carry light loads, such as groceries, up and down slopes and
stairs; and has an obstacle-detection system to assist users in navigation. The goal of the
STRIDE system is to provide a finished prototype that addresses all of these needs. The
three main components of STRIDE are the rollator, the stair climbing mechanism, and the
safety sensors. The development of these components is outlined in Section 3, the final design
details of the finished device are listed in Section 4, and the testing and evaluation of these
components is summarized in Section 5.

templateVisual.interaction: Teal hover with a bright ice-blue click state.

projects.summary: In many powered electric devices, batteries supply power to drive the motors and the electronics. As the devices consume the energy, the battery gets depleted over time, requiring recharging after certain thresholds are crossed. As a result, the available charge at any given moment must be tracked to alert the users to charge the batteries. The system may also shut down some functionality when the available capacity goes beyond a specified minimum. The STRIDE  (S

subsections.description: I enjoy creating projects from the ground up as well as reverse engineering existing ones, taking them from the initial idea to full realization. This often involves working on everything from project constraints and budget to design, implementation, testing, and validation.
